CURRENT AFFAIRS,

"Mr. Bung of Ennis is cross with Mr. William Red-
mond. One would think—and probably Bung does
think so—that the drink shops owned the constituency.
A special meeting of the Ennis Urban Council met to
consider Mr. Redmond’s Local Veto Bill. The requisi-
tion for it was signed by Mr. O’Moloney, Mr. Ahern,
and Mr. Capon, three free and enlightened Bungs, the
last-named is a Cockney, we understand. M.
O’Moloney stated that it was the publicans of Ennis
 who elected Mr. William Redmond to Parliament !

. My. Capon moved a resolution that resolved, amox_xgsb
other things, that Mr. W. Ret‘imondi had "’forfe)ted
the confidence of this Council.”” Mr. O’Moloney
~ seconded, and remarked that Mr. W. Redmond wanted

_ to revenge himsel ‘¢ for it was well known that it was
the liquor trade defeated him when he went to be High
Sheriff of Dublin.”” There was a plea for nlntxldness', b‘}‘,’
Mr. O’Moloney would not hear of it. - ‘' Nothing,
said this Bung, could be too strong, considering he

proposes to take away your living.”’

3 y ‘What, not even a
glass of Fair Day whiskey neat ?

It appears that there were only six persons at the
meeting, and that ““nearly all”’ of those were in-
terested in the carrying out of the great Irish Bung
tribute of some £14,000,000 annually. Capon evidently
pleaded eloquently for the Bungs and himself. What
was Bung to do? People were preaching the gospel of
stopping the tide of emigration, “but,”” asked Mr.
Capon, ‘‘ what would be left to poor families but to
go to America.”’” Mr. Capon ought to go further and
suggest that every intending emigrant should be offered
a new licence to open a Bungery as an inducement to
keep him at home! The great meeting of six Ennis
Urban Councillors, ‘“nearly all’’ interested in the
drink traffic, finally decided to write to Mr. Redmond
and adjourned. We wonder to whose house did they
adjourn, or did each one go his own way?

We learn that of the twelve Ennis Councillors nine
are interested in the drink traffic. TInstead of the Ennis
Council losing confidence in Mr. Redmond, supposing
for a change that the Ennis electorate lost confidence
in its Council and threw out, at its next election, a job
lot of, say, six Bungs, including O’Moloney and Capon !

Mr. Capon who stated that ‘‘ they all knew that in
a small ward, such as they had there in the town, of
100 electors, it might be a poor class ward, and the
majority of the people there could blackmail them for
porter.””  We wonder for which ward is this Mr.
Capon a representative? And if there is anything in
his contention would it not be well to have a regulation
that no bungery owner should be eligible for local
government in Ennis where these ‘‘ blackmailers’’ are
feared by Mr. Capon. For surely if ‘“blackmailers,”
comprising a majority of the electorate of a ward, could
give trouble to Mr. Capon, it would appear that an
unscrupulous Bung—if there be such a person extant—
could buy the votes of these ‘‘blackmail’’ type of
electors at so many pints a vote at election time?

After all, Bung is not the people even in Ennis. We
hear that Mr. W. Redmond, in this matter, has the
sympathy and support of the artisans and labourers of
Ennis, as well as the Gaelic League, and the Ennis
Trade and Labour Association. Moreover, we believe
that a great many people have votes besides the nine
Bungs of the Ennis Council. No doubt young Mooney
or Joe Nolan or Pat O’Brien or Tim Harrington would
suit the Bung’s of Ennis better than Mr.  William
Redmond as member for the division in which Mr.
O’Moloney, the Milesian, and Mr. Capon, the Cockney,
retail porter.

Sergeant James Dolan, R.I.C., of Belleek, where the
pottery comes from, has scored well over his, or his
reputed, “amiable qualities.”” This “ minion "’ it ap-
pears, accomplished the extraordinary task of serving
twenty-eight -years as a peeler “ without a blemish on
his .character.’”” This paragon who, in the course of
twenty-eight long years, remained a'loyal bobby  with-
out a blemish on his character,”” was recently presented
with an address and with a purse of sovereigns: A
peeler’s lot in Ireland is a soft one, with the prospect of
a gold lining at the end. The purse of “golden’’
sovereigns was contributed to by “his friends of all
creeds and classes.” .

In many respects it would be more agreeable to be a

bobby. than an editor. The text of the address from.
“ the people of Belleek and district,”’ xuns as follows:—
. On learning with deep regret of your retiring from the police
force, and your departure from amongst us, we could not let yho
opportunity pass without showing, in some small way, our high
appreciation of your many good qualities in the position. -which

98

Fou held, so faithful to your duty, and so agrecable to every creed
and dlass of the community. We have known you now for over
seven years, and, during that time, while upholding the law with
credit to yourself, vou never used such ‘as a source of annoyance
to the people. Friction, from time.to time, naturally arose, which
was never taken advantage of by you for self notoriety, but al-
lowed to calm down where at all possible, and, now that you are
retiring to oivil life, we earnestly wish you success in whatover
sphere of life you may select to follow during the remaining years
~hich you may be permitted to share the ups and downs of this
restless globe of ours.

This address that refers to “this restless Globe of ours”
—they ought to stick a pin in the Belleek pottery works
and make them a little less restful—is signed on behalf
of the Committee by James Cleary, Chairman; John
Cassidy, Hon. Secretary; John M‘Gree, Treasurer.

Peeler Dolan dutifully replied. The bobby accepted
the sovereigns “ with pleasure ”’—we would be tempted
%o take sovereigns in that spirit ourselves, though we
are nothing so “tony ’’ as bobbies—well we can under-
stand that, a bobby is only human after all. These
ridiculous testimonials are enough to make peelers con-
ceited and crease their hair at both sides of their heads.
Listen to this “fine writing’’ from peeler Dolan’s reply—

I am thoroughly conscious that the motives which actuated you
were not prompted solely by any merits which you are good enough
to say T possess, but rather emanated from your own innate kind-
ness of heart and generous disposition. However, I am proud to
learn, from the complimentary tones of your Address, that my
mission hero as a Sergeant of Polico for tho last soven vears has
resulted in giving fair play to all without fear, favour, or affec-
tion. I may say, without trace of egotism, that I have striven to
do my duty in as fair and impartial a manner as I possibly could;
indeed, as a matter of fact, this district is =0 peaceable and law-
abiding that police duties were reduced to a minimum.

The italics are ours. So the peeler has a “ mission,”’
has he! Does the bobby seek to place himself on a level
with our “mystics >’ and our “ poets.”’

At the conclusion of the presentation to the Belleek
peeler the Company were “hospitably entertained.’’
‘We wonder how much “ net ”’ went to peeler Dolan out
of the purse of sovereigns? For “ toast after toast hav-
ing been drank in honour of the sergeant ’’ (for which
toasts the poor peeler had to pay), the proceedings
ended. When will these ridiculous peeler testimonials
end?

We are not much interested in the breach of promise
of marriage case in which Miss Robinson got a verdict
of £270 damages against the Rev. Mr. Smyth, “ Saved’’
Rector of St. Donard’s, Bloomfield. Those who read
the evidence in the daily papers will probably think
that the jury left the reverend cad down lightly. We
would be, indeed, sorry to think that there were many
persons of the so-called “ Church of Ireland ’’ like Mr.
Smyth. One feature of the case struck us particularly.
Miss Robinson, who got £270 damages, swore that Mr.
Smyth said that if he married her his fellow- Saved ’’
clergy would not call upon her! One “Saved’’ clergy
told him that the young lady was not class enough for
Rey. Mr. Smyth. How “tony’’ the parsons must be,
to be sure. One might almost think that they were all
“souper ”’ ministers from the Divine School of the mud-
throwers’ University they are so “ classy!”’ According
to evidence the lady’s mother said to the parson—“I
thought it was to cure souls you worked, and not for
calling on you or recognising me.””  Evidently Mrs.
Robinson was a simple woman.

We suppose that the Cape Times is the Alf Foz of
South Africa. It has a London correspondent, and we
find in the London letter, which appeared in its issue
of January 26, a copy of which a reader of the LEADER
in Cape Colony has kindly sent us, the following para-
gra}?h which, we are sure, will be read with mingled
feelings of amusement and amazement by our readers—

l(x:. Redmond says that armed revolt. would be justifiable; most
of Sir Henry's colleagues are determined that Ireland- shall not
8plit away from England; while thinking men, who live quietly
in Ireland, emong the peqph, like Mr. T. W.: Rolleston, who used
to-be a_Separatist, are oconyinced that the Irish people are com-
pletely (unfitted to govern more than their own internal affaire.

« They 816 ignorant, and worse than ignorant, ruled by i !

THE LEADER.

ts, who Freeman  and, ‘the -one-man’s-ha’peny

APRIL 7, 1906,
are unscrupulous, often bad men, and always tyrannical. A ritet
thinks nothing of knooking an old man senseless who has dared
to_differ from him in politics; a priest thinks nothing of appro-
priating funds given for other purposes to building or keeping up
seminaries for priests. The little stories collected in Mr. Gy
Moore's volume, * An Untilled Field,” are entirely true: still, and
it is @ book which should bo read by persons who think of giving
power to the Irish. And why, indeed, should wo put anything s
dangorous into tho hands of people so extraordinarily ignosant
that when their daughters come up to service in Dublin they in-
Variably attempt o get upstairs on their hands and knees, and
have to be taught, literally, to walk up? ]

If this is the sort of stuff that the Cape Times treats its
readers to with regard to Ireland, we wonder what does:
it say of the Boer. Perhaps the Boers walk up stairs
on their heads, and roast alive those who disagree with
them in politics!

We notice that there are ‘“high words’’ in Lord
Bung’s evening paper Buff concerning Green,; what we
say is—there is a pair of them in it. Tt is all over a
British horse race called the ‘“ Grand National,”” what-
ever that is. The Zwvening Herald and the Evening
Mail each claims, we understand, that it was the first
to accomplish the Irish national work of giving
““ Bookies’ ”’ dupes the result of the British race. Ac-
cording to Buff a G'reen representative of the name of.
Nixon ‘“ was deputed to lurk round the back doors of
the Mail and to time its issue of the Stop Press Edition’’
This man Nixon, of Green, bought two copies of Buff
and handed them to one John F. McNamara, also of
Green ; and says Buff—“At once these eager, if not very
dignified, journalists hurried off to a magistrate, before
whom they made a solemn declaration that they secured
their copies at 2.53 p.m.”” Later on Lord Bung’s Bujff
refers to Mr. McNamara, of Green, as ‘‘the distin-
guished journalist who appears to have been lurking
round a neighbouring corner.”” We suppose in due
time Buff’s young man and G7reen’s young man will
‘“make it up’’ at the next ‘‘ God-save-the-King’
dinner of the British Institute of Journalists, and per-
haps they may fall on one another’s necks when the
Scotch public-house lyric, ‘‘ For he’s a jolly good fel-
low,”” rends the atmosphere.

Lord Bung’s Buff evidently does not regard the
honour of the Independent’s Green as above suspicion.
Buff says—‘‘ We know something of the Herald’s con-
ception of time, as well as its standard of honour—
hence our contemptuous disbelief in its laboured and
ludicrous affidavits.”” Why not let the sporting tipster
of Buff and the betting Johnnie of Green fight it out
between them? Pink could send its two-to-one-bar-one
reporter to record the number of times that Green
‘“got one home on the beak,’” and how often Buff drew.
Green’s ‘‘ claret.”” There is nothing like hurrying up
with nation-making !

We have received the first number of a new and en-
larged series of the Westmeath Independent. The
Westmeath Independent was originally only a four-page
paper. It is now, we understand, the largest provin-
cial newspaper in Ireland, comprising 16 pages, and
what is worthy of note, 16 pages of paper manufactured
at Ballyclare. We are glad to. see that our contem-
porary devotes a large proportion of its space to Irish
Ireland matters, and in an editorial announcement
concerning some forthcoming contributions we notice
that mention is made of an Irish play written by M.
Waldron, of Ballyhaunis. Ireland has need of journals
viewing the life of the country from a general stand-
point, criticising and suggesting, but it also needs ably
and honestly-conducted papers doing equally useful
work in more local spheres. We congratulate the editor
and management of our contemporary upon the success
which the present great expansion of their paper
indicates.

About five years ago we had much to say about Sun
day horse-racing. in Dublin. In due time the abuse
ceased ; but now we find that the Ashtown Sunday races
are 'being renewed. Of course :the “truly nati

‘aqothqr-m»a.n’s—ha.'penuy Independent has not opened a
tampaign on the resurrection of the Sunday race meet-
ings. When fighting is to be done the job is left to
LEAD_ER 5 when the fight is won then the LEADER is not
mentioned, and the Xrceman and such papers commence
to work their clappers. _When the great question of
Ir{sh Insurance for the Irish people that the LEADER
agitated when the so-called “ Patriotic’’ and the un-
fortunate “ National ”’ underwent their recent develop-
ments, is advanced sufficiently all the *tame ’’ partial
followers of the LEaDER like the Freeman and the In-
dependent will commence ponderous articles with some
Phrase such as “ As we have always said, etc.”

But to return to the Sunday Ashtown races. We
stopped these abuses before, and we are confident that
we can stop them again. Who are “running’’ these
races? In whose interest are they being flung like a
shadow across the Inmish Catholic Sunday? It is bad
enough to have Dan Tallon desirous to make his bond
slaves do servile work on a holy day of the Church on
St. Patrick’s Day, without putting the “bookie’’ and
the jockey at work on Sundays. If the people who
“run’’ the Sunday Ashtown races persist in their work
we will persist in looking after them. We may go
there one Sunday and write the whole thing up, names
of the “ sports’’ included!

Here is an account of a Lisburn concert which a con-
tributor sends us:—‘ On Friday night, 30th March,
Lisburn was the scene of an epoch-making concert, held
in aid of a local charity, and in which the coon figures
very prominently. A lengthy report of this coon con-
cert occupies a conspicuous place in the Saturday issue
of the Belfast Catholic and National paper that boy-
cotted St.” Patrick. We are informed that the pro-
gramme was delightfully varied, Varied, I admit; de-
lightfully—a scholastic distinction is necessary here.
The singing of ‘Billy Byrne of Ballymanus,” we are
told, was followed by ‘a red-nosed turn every whit as
good as any on the Alhambra stage.” What a red-nosed
turn is I don’t pretend to know; but I'm perfectly cer-
tain that a red nose, even without the turn, is anything
but an enviable feature. And this red-nosed turn was
every whit as good as any on the Alhambra stage.
Now ! There’s the standard of excellence to which we,
up Lisburn way, aspire; we can produce a red-nosed
turn in St. Joseph’s Hall, Lisburn, just as good as any
in the Alhambra. But the walls of St. Joseph’s Hall
must have been shaken to their foundations by the
applause in which the pent up feelings of a raptured
audience found vent at the conclusion of ‘I wouldn’t
leave my little wooden hut,”  Good-bye, my lady love,’
and ¢ All the little ducks went quack, quack, quack.’
With the first of these I am not very familiar, but some-
how it gives one the semsation of sea-air and clowns.
It may, however, be an Elizabethan lyric; for a cer-
tainty, that line as it stands is Spenserian. And those
little ducks that went quack, quack, quack! How weally
deloightful!| * These items were interspersed with
‘0’Domhnaill Abu’ and ‘When shall the day break in
Erin ’—most likely this is the variation of the pro-
gramme. The pathos must have reached the extreme
when the strains of that ‘When shall the day’ fell upon
ears still tingling with the quack, quack, quack of the
little ducks. Three gentlemen then gave a fine rex}der-
ing of ¢ Way down.upon the Swanee ribber,” ¢ Eileen
Alannah,” and ‘The dear little shamrock ’—another
variation. We are told that one of these gentlemen.—
the Swanee ribber one, I take it—was ‘in an excruciat-
ingly funny make-up, consisting of a b’nmlegs hat, a
red wig, a red nose and clown costume. —Irish News,
that boycotted St. Patrick. That the said gentleman
was excruciating I have not th(_a least dopbt ; that he was
funny in a peculiar sense I might admit too, but there
is another side to this: there is a sad aspect of this
sort of thing, that an Irish concert—TI mean a concert

"in Treland—held in aid of a local charity and presided

e local clergy, should include in its programme
’t leave. my little wooden hut,” “Good-bye,
"“The little ducks went quack, quack,

with the brimless hat, the red wig, red nose and clown
costume. One feels the more aggravated by this kind
of trash when one considers the state of unstable equili-
brium in which we are at present in this country with
regard to the stage and the concert room, and adverts
for a moment to the struggle that is being made to
cleanse the Irish mind from Music Hall dirt and re-
create Irish msthetic taste. Amnother item which I find
in this coon programme is a coon song and dance, with
which the audience was delighted, by one Joe Meleady,
‘negroistic artiste in black, and mandoline soloist,” says
the Irish News. I think I know what a mandoline
soloist is, but what kind of animal is a ‘negroistic artiste
in black’? Miss Dora McConvey is down as an Irish
singer. We are not told whether she sang an Irish
song, but if she did, how the queer old language must
have grated on the Lisburn ear! Just one more tit-bit
from the Zrish News on the Lisburn audience: ¢ The
audience applauded Mr. Denis Glasgow, Mr. Cahal
O’Byrne, and Mr. John Diffin, who contributed Anglo-
Irish items, every whit as enthusiastically as they.ap-
plauded the ladies and gentlemen who contributed the
purely English items.”  (Italics mine). That speaks
for itself.”

There was a time when events were only recorded in
song and story. That was before the days of the cine-
matograph. The Irish Revival has, to a great extent,
synchronised with the development of that great inven-
tion; and we are glad to see it put to the use of re-
calling important phases in the march of the Irish move-
ment. Messrs. Thomas Mayne, of Lord Edward St.,
Dublin, have, we learn, secured a series of very success-
ful cinematograph pictures of this year’s Language Pro-
cession, which they recently showed at the Gaelic
League Offices in O’Connell St. Their pictures were
taken as the Procession passed along the quays to Smith-
field, and include the principal and most striking fea-
tures of the big display.

Another firm in this line, Messrs. Thomas Mason,
Dame Street, Dublin, have been bringing some
activities of Irish industry within the range of the
magic lantern. We have received a copy of their
pamphlet with the suggestive title, *“ Ireland at Work,”’
by which we notice that they have obtained a series of
90 slides descriptive of the premises and processes of
manufacture of such well-known Irish firms as Good-
body’s, Rathborne’s, Winstanley’s, John M‘Donnell
and Co., Atkinson’s, Varian’s, Kapp and Petersun’s,
ete.

‘We do not see any column, half-column, quarter-
column, or paragraph in Irish in the Connacht Cham-
pion of March 3lst. The Connacht Champion is pub-
lished by Mr. John O’Donnell, M.P., one of poor Mr.
O’Brien’s men. Bub if there is no Irish, there is over
a column of stage Anglo-Irish—or perhaps the column
and a bit from “Paddy Rambler ’ is not stage Augln-
Trish, but the vernacular of the Galway “ patriots.’”’
Here is a specimen frem the issue of this paper that has
no Irish matter :—“ We had guges in town lasht week,
and shure ave the hadn’t them peelers along with them ;
ave there was much more ave thim in Ireland than was
in Galway at the Assizes there musht be a quare lot ave
thim in it ontirely.””  Judging by the spelling one
might almost suspect that “ Paddy Rawbler”’ was an
Arts’ graduate of the so-called University in College
Green. Here is another specimen from this article in
our Connacht contemporary:—“ Who is this Micky
Glin that kot Mr. Krooley? Begorra he’s very like a
Japanee, small and yallaw, and he rides a quare soort
ave a kisikel, that does be puffin’ goin’ along, and his
feet sthayin’ shteady. The game purtekshun societe
must be badly off for some wan to luck afther the birds
ave the air when'they had to go to’'Japanee for Micky
Glin.” ~We protest against the stage Irishman;. there
is. a vigorous campaign against him even in the United
States, yet here'is this sort of ignorant drivel welcomed
in a paper run' by poor Mr. O’Brien’s John O’Donnell.

 What a pretty country we would have if William and

his men were dictating!

« By kind permission ”’ of one Colonel Mellor an en-
tentainment was held in the Tommy Atkins’ barracks
at Omagh in aid of the District Nursing Association.
Could not the promoters find a better place than a
Tommy Atkins’ barracks to hold the entertainment ?
<« All classes’’ were present under the hospitable roof
of . Tommy, and the entertainment, needless to say,
wound up with the party tune which, in this country,
really means “ Damn the Nationalists!”’ Full Private
B. O’Kane sang “ My Pretty Zulu Lu.”” We wonder
was she anything to any of the dozen Zulus who
were exccuted recently? Tommy is a better shot at an
execution than at an affair like the Modder River. Dr.
T. Connor sang “ My Old Kentucky Home,”’ and Ser-
geant J. M‘Evoy warbled  Walking Home with Ange-
line.”” How “classy,”’ to be sure! The doctors up
Omagh way would appear to be exceptionally inane as
far as taste in lyrics go, for a Dr. A. Barnett sang
“Coon, coon, coon.”” ~ We have heard of many silly
titles for songs, but it would be hard to beat the title
of Dr. Barnett’s lyric.

It was about time that there was an Irish-Ireland
entertainment in Monaghan. By all accounts the con-
cert, held under the joint auspices of the Gaelic League
and the G.A.A., was a success from every point of view.
Monaghan has a Catholic hall—what every town in Ire-
land ought to have; it also has an Irish-Ireland organist,
Mr. Florence B. Macaulay, and it is a pity there are
not more organists of his type in the country. We trust
that Mr. Macaulay and his associates will organise a
few more Irish concerts, and so stir up the Irish move-
ment in the Monaghan district.

‘What we may be pardoned for describing as an excel-
lent suggestion, is made in the course of the “ Gaelic
League Echoes’’ in our contemporary the Waterford
Star. The suggestion is as follows:— Those of our
readers who wish to let some of our highly respectables
see themselves as others see them could not do better
than send them a copy of “ Tom O’Kelly ’’ as an Easter
Gift. Ridicule will kill where argument and remon-
strance will have no effect.”

The pressure on our space is very great, and we have
been compelled to hold over a large number of articles
and several letters this week.

THE LEADER.

AprIL 7, 1906.
CORRESPONDENCE.

A TRALEE INDUSTRY.

Sir,—In reference to am article on Tralee by “ Veri-
tas,”” which recently appeared in the columms of your
paper, and which, while briefly touching on the indus-
trial progress which the town has made in recent years,
went on to give a list of the leading merchants there, for
whom was claimed much of the credit for the prosperity
of the place.

On reading this it appeared somewhat strange that no
mention whatever was made of Messrs. Galvin and Son,
one of the most prominent and certainly one of the most
successful firms in the town—more especially so, per-
haps, as it was they who started the Kerry Knitting
Tactory in Tralee some fifteen years ago, and which since
then has come to be so well known even outside of Ire-
land, taking its place, as it does, amongst the most, flour-
ishing enterprises started in the country for many years,
while at the same time probably contributing in no small
way to the industrial prosperity of Tralee.

Furthermore, when one reflects on the amount, of em-
ployment such an undertaking is likely to give, there is
an extra interest added on, apart from the usual feeling
which an ordinary successful financial venture is met
with. The mere fact that on an average 80 hands find
constant employment in the factory is in itself a suffi-
cient inducement to keep even a certain number of the
people at home in their own country who otherwise
would probably have had to seek for their common live-
lihood elsewhere, while also the constant and increasing
demand that there is from all parts for Kerry Knitting
Tactory goods, goes a long way to testifying to the ex-
cellence of the work which can be done by the Inish
people when they stay at home and when there is a
chance that work can be found for them there.—T re-
main, Sir, truly yours,

BUNG’S DEFIANCE.

+¢ T REGRET we do not meet under brighter circum-
stances,”” so said Daniel Tallon, keeper of the
Magic Button, at the annual meeting of the Central
Committee of the Liquor Trade of Ireland. The more
Daniel and his like regret, the more reason for rejoicing
on the part of this Bung-soaked country. Perhaps
Dan Tallon did not make a great fortune in his bungery
on St. Patrick’s Day, clse he might have been in a more
cheerful mood. ‘ Moreover,”” said the keeper of the
Magic Button, “moreover, with the advent of a new
Government there has uprisen a new movement of so-
called ‘reform.””” We suppose that down deep in the
secret, heart of many a Bung in Ireland there is a regret
that the Tory Coercionist Government is not in power!
After all, what is Ireland to “the Trade?’ “It would be
idle,”” said the Magic Button man, it would be idle to
deny that at the moment the outlook for the Trade is
serious.””  We are glad to hear it; and what we say is
““more o’ that to it.”” Poor Dan, the keeper of the
Magic Button, and one-time Lord Mayor of Mudlin, is
inconsolable.  Said he:—“On the one hand we are
threatened with Local Veto, on the other hand with
Total Sunday Closing and Early Saturday Closing,-
while a Bill has again been introduced for the closing of
licensed premises on St. Patrick’s Day. Besides, there
are the proposals of Sir Andrew Reed and his ¢ Round
Table’ Conference, to which the representatives of the
Trade were never invited.””  Pity the sorrows of the
poor persecuted Bungs.  There ought to be a testi-
monial got up for them without delay. Perhaps they
would send round the hat from house to house on next
Mr. Bung’s Day—the 17th March! Such philanthro-
pists and philosophers should not be left to soak, un-
aided, our down-trodden people. We learn from Dan
that the membership of this particular Bung organisa~
tion is not what it ought to be, We take it that as D
Daniel sponged on St. Patrick, so many of “the Trade” >
are sponging on Daniel and his friends by allowing them
to keep up the organisation,\they remaining content to’
take whatever advantages acerue from it. It is prob-
ably a case of—set a sponger to sponge on a sponger.
The Magic Button keeper sighed in sorrow over the ne-
farious Veto Bill of Mr. William Redmond. Reading’
the speech one would almost think that Mr. Daniel
Tallon took a sup of his fieriest malt before he referred
to the St. Patrick’s Day Closing Bill. The very recol-:
lection of that vile proposal of Mr. Q’Mara appears to
have sent an electric shock through Tallon as if some-
one had touched the magic button to ‘which his body
was switched. - “To the Bill,” said this Bung, “to the "
Bill which hasbeen introduced for the compulsory clos-
ing of licensed houses on St. Patrick’s Day, it is our
intention to offer the strongest possible oppasition.
our opinion, not only is this restriction uncalled for and
unnecessary, but it carries with it a humiliating reflec
tion.”” 2 v - 5
Now, are the Irish Parliamentary Party in the
“hollow of the hand’’ of Mr. Bung? If not,
make Mr. O’Mara’s Bill a party question, an
Mr. Pat O’Brien and young Mooney of “Th
in their places? Certainly the closing

‘on 8t. Patrick’s Day ought to be a national question,
‘and fellows like young Mooney, who have pledged them-

~ selves to “sit, act, and vote’’ with the Irish Party,
will have to forget that they are Bungs first, and Irish
M.P.’s afterwards, when the Party make the matter a
party question. Bung, in the person of Tallon, the
keeper of the Magic Button, says that he will give Mr.
O’Mara’s Bill the strongest possible opposition. The
plain question now is—will the Irish Party, as a party,
throw in their lot with the enlightened elements of the
Irish nation, or will they elect to climb down on the
dark Bung aside, and betray the nation in the interests
of the drink shop?

A Mr. Patrick Crowley from Cork—we have no doubt
he is a “tried and true patriot '’—proposed a resolution
which was unanimously passed by the Bung patriots
assembled consigning Bung’s Bond Slaves to hard labour
on the day that, by a stroke of irony, one Russell, who
occupies the position of secretary, once held by Mike
M‘Carthy “the Catholic,’”’ stated was made a national
holiday. Bung is a man of sentiment. We give the
full text of the resolution:— That we protest against
the attempt being made to pass into law a Bill for the
compulsory closing of all licensed houses in Ireland on
St. Patrick’s Day, inasmuch as we believe such a re-
striction is uncalled for and unnecessary, and that we
regret the action of Mr. James O’Mara, J.P., and those
associated with the movement, being of opinion that
they are casting upon the character of the Irish people
a humiliating reflection, which the circumstances in con-
nection with the celebration of the National Festival in
recent years have proved to be entirely without justifi-
cation.”” 'We italicised the part that came clean out of
the philanthropic and national section of Bung’s giz-
zard—for it might be a libel on “the Trade’’ to sug-
gest that Mr. Bung had a heart.

CATHOLICS AND THE ROYAL UNIVERSITY.

"[ “HE Royal University is ruled by its Senate. The
Senate, in the words of the Charter, has “the
entire management of, and superintendence over, the
affairs, concerns, and property of the said University.’

It has “full power to make and alter Statutes, Rules,

and Ordinances.”’

When complete, the Senate consists of 36 Members
and a Chancellor. The original idea was that 18 of the
36 should be Catholics and 18 non-Catholics. - This
would be “equality,”’ but the equality was destroyed
by the determination that the Chancellor should always
be a Protestant, and this determination has been
steadily carried eut. Of the 36 Members of the Senate
six are eleated by “ Convocation,” other vacancies, as
they occur, are filled up by the Government.  Until
1899 the six Senators elected by  Convocation,’” were,

< . Catholics; but on the completion of his term by Dr.
- O’Sullivan—a Catholic—the Presbyterians put forward
Dr. Walton Brown and elected him.  This destroyed
“the balance of denominations,”’ and non-Catholics
have ever since been in a majority on the Senate, even
irrespective of the vote of the Chancellor, who, as we
have said, is always a Protestant. At present there are
16 Catholics and 19 non-Catholics on the Senate. Let
ug now see how “ Convocation ’’ is constituted. .

has to fill up a form, which he can obtain by calling at
the University, or by writing for it to “ The Clerk of
‘Convocation, Royal University, Dublin.” Filling up the
ing it duly witnessed, and sending the pound

As we have said, the calendar has the names of some

By T. Garroway Rice.

F Ireland, unlike every other country with a similar
I population—whether independent, quasi-indepen-
dent, or colonial—is at present without a single Irish
Life Insurance Company, the fact is not impossible of
explanation—or, indeed, difficult to understand by any-
body possessing a lengthened experience of the country,
and some knowledge of its recent history.

An aged relative of mine who entered the service of
the old firm of Todd, Burns and Co. some seventy years
ago, informed me that at that time the only Catholics
in the employ were the van-drivers and the porters; and
that it was the same in all similar establishments, and
in everything else. Possessing neither newspapers nor
literature—except Ward’s Cantos and Cobbett’s History
of the Reformation—their position all round was quite
impossible of realisation, except by persons over sixty
years of age, and with a varied experience. I may add
that my old friend—who occupied a high position in the
firm—was refused a partnership solely on the ground of
his being a Catholic. T mention the fact on account of
its being so very well known to his friends. Whenever
he spoke of the commercial position of his co-religionists
in Ireland, his astonishment was always—not that they
were S0 backward—but that they had so far advanced.

My own experience hardly dates so far back as that of
this recently-deceased nonagenarian. . But it goes b:
fairly far. About thirty years ago I was sent from
burgh to take the place of another Scotchman
Dublin office of a large Insurance Company. I w
surprised to know that the secretary and his pred:
were Scotch and Protestant; bu

Now in reference to Fire, Life, and other forms of in-

Widows’, the Alliance, the Sun, and the North British

lodge them. A further adverse circumstance is the
great proportion of the people of Ireland belonging to
the small farmer class, and therefore to the non-insuring

CONFESSION OF AN O'GROWNEYITE.

ENT should be a time of truthfulness. ’Tis there-
fore I make confession. I sit down upon a
Wednesday evening to recount the passions of youth,
the disillusionment of later years. I was reading the
statement in “ Imaal’s ’’ article about every man being
afraid of the whole truth on some subject, when ib
struck me that there was just one educational subject on
which a great number of us are very much afraid of the
whole truth, and that is the study of Irish. I have
often thought that the cynic might find not a little
material in that splendid funeral which we gave Father
O’Growney some time ago, and the fine monument we
erected over his grave. For so many of us, he is the
Irish writer, the interpreter of the great unknown. He
was our first love; we have remained faithful to him
ever since; and we express a melancholy affection over
his tomb—c4 pe peap mop, say we in sweet remi-
niscence of the glorious days of our enthusiasm.

Of course, gentle reader, this does not apply to you.
You belong to the majority; your efforts at the study
of Irish have been crowned with complete success;
you are the average man, and not, like myself, one of
the minority of failures; when your bi-lingual children
return merrily from school—they are educated by that
great Irish-TIrish order “ the ists ”’—you and your
spouse discuss the composition of the dinner and the
evening’s doings in Irish. And when “——’" who
writes for “don ,”’ drops over for a chat, you are
not satisfied with the preliminary Irish rally, but keep
on playing that way all through the game. T admire
you, my dear sir, (or 4 ¢ape), and it is a splendid sight
to see you marching in your thousands in the language
procession. E

But, as is not unusual in such circumstances, I am
alittle slow in coming to the point of my own confes-
sion. Now it is quite a long time ago since I started
Irish, long before THE LEADER was heard of, in days
when the Gaelic League was quite an obscure body.
I think I anticipated nearly all my more immediate
friends and acquaintances, who have since commenced
Gaelic, not less than fifty in number at a low computa-

HE art of satirising living personalities in works

that profess to be fiction isnot art of a high order.
e have had an example of it in tho Seething Pot, and
" we have a still more unwelcome specimen in Hyacinth, a
work by the same author, which is just now receiving
the attentions of the reviewers. The writer—a Protes-
tant clergyman who poses, we understand, as an Irish
Trelander—has a lofty contempt for our current ideals
of patriotism, and an unusual measure of Protestant
_scorn for the ministers and institutions of ‘our national
religion. The book which these gifts have enabled him
o produce will be a delight to all with whom Mr.
chael M‘Carthy is a prophet. 3 :
Qur friends of the physical force party have a promi-
t position in the pillory which Mr. Birmingham—
he name the author assumes—has set up. We

have ourselves used a certain freedom in our criticisms of
this party. But we have not qualified them as knaves
and swindlers, and herein we differ from Mr. Birming-
ham. It is not, however, necessary for us to undertake
the defence of the gentlemen and ladies atbacked ; they
are quite able to take care of themselves.

The hero of the book, Mr. Hyacinth Conneally, son
of a “Souper’’ clergyman in Connemara, who enters
the Divinity School of Trinity College, quarrels with the
professor, and retires; then, after desultory consulta-
tions with the priest of his native parish as to his future,
joins Miss Augusta Goold, the heroine of the advanced
party, in her efforts to raise a fighting contingent to aid
the Boers, but discovering that the leader of the pro-
posed expedition has taken service in the cause in order
to escape prosecution for forgery, renounces his revolu-
tionary enthusiasm, and subsides into the prosaic posi-
tion of clerk and traveller for a Western woollen factory.
Hardly one of the individuals whose acquaintance Hya-
cinth makes in his career which leads to the factory
office is other than a fool or a knave. Miss Augusta
Goold and her followers, in particular, are a sorry lot.
Their characters and powers are in ludicrous contrast
with their political projects.

Their one substantial achievement is a scuffle in the
Rotunda in which Hyacinth, after the manner of the
corner-boy rather than of the approved hero of fiction,
gets the better of his opponent by hitting him with a
stone. It requires no ingenuity to recognise in some of
the professedly imaginary characters of the book the liv-
ing personalities whom the author is lampooning. As
for plot, there is nothing in the volume which deserves
the name ; the incidents are mere parodies of occurrences
with which the newspapers have made us familiar. -

Throughout the book the author manifests a venomous
hatred for the religious institutions of the majority of
the Irish people; his association with the Gaelic League
has not, it would appear, enabled him to understand the
religious element in the Irish character, or sympathise
with its manifestations.  Of this, however, we have,
perhaps, no right to complain. Bigots are as free as
more enlightened people to write books, and if they use
their liberty they will write as they think. But what
we are warranted in condemning even in the most en-
venomed bigot is that he should pass beyond mere pre-
judice or ignorance, and write of those with whom he
does not agree what he knows, or by easy inquiry may
know, to be grossly calumnious. This Mr. Birmingham
has done. And this offence is so committed that no
claim for freedom in fiction can be urged to excuse it.

He represents as the rival of the woollen factory for
which Hyacinth travels, a factory maintained by a
Catholic sisterhood.  This rival factory he calls
“Robeen.””  He locates it in Mayo; it is engaged im  *
the manufacture of tweeds; and it has received grants
from the Congested Districts Board for the establish-
ment of the industry it carries on. Now there is in Ire-
land only one institution to which these details apply.
Mr. Birmingham might at once have written  Foxford’’
instead of “Robeen;’’ with the descriptive particulars
added the latter no more conceals the object of his attack -
than would the former. 3

In last month’s number of 7'he New Ireland Review,
the allegations which Mr. Birmingham makes against
the Foxford nuns are dealt with seriatum. He accuses
them of “sweating’’ the labour they employ; this is
shown by the actual figures of the wages bill to be a base-

less insinuation. He charges them with spending money 2
on a sumptuous, conventual building; it is proved that
they content themselves with a cottage enlarged by sheds
of wood and iron. He represents them as appealing to,
Catholic bigotry in order to draw away Catholic cus-
tomers from Protestant manufacturers; it is demon
strated that their goods are as freely taken by Protestan
traders as by Catholic, and that it is therefore the :
of their goods that secure them a market.
That the author has some smartness of style we
not deny. But before attaining popularity in Irel;

with Mr. Davitt. It reminds me of the paper-
h}mts we used to have as school-boys. I have pursued
him so far and wide in his excursions, because I wanted
to show up his extraordinary faculty for wild inaccuracy.
The question he began to discuss is the question of
Primary Education in England. But he directly led a
wild chase on to the School question in America, Aus-
tralia, Belgium, France, etc. ; then one irrelevant bound
back to Adrian IV.; then on to the Veto question;
then Keogh and Sadleir politics; then the Queen’s Col-
leges; the National Schools; Trinity College; Oxford
and Cambridge; the Synod of Maynooth ; the Synod of
1 Thurles; the Synod of Baltimore, and the sending of a
Papal Legate in hot haste to make an American Bishop
behave himself ; the disregard of Dr. O’Dwyer, and of all
ecclesiastics, in fact, for the secular education of Catho-
lics; and so on, and so.on, through acres of letter-writ-
ing you can track the trail of his ink, which should

FRIEND has sent to me the Cawilta Cattolica for
the 17th March. Thatis the well-known
Fortnightly Review published in Rome by the Italian
Jesuit Fathers. In that copy sent to me I have read
the first of a series of sketches entitled “The Green
Isle.”” Under fictitious names, this writer, lately ar-
rived in Ireland, proposes to give his impressions of
what he has seen. The following translation from his
first paper will tell you what his knowledge of Irish
history is like:— Cromwell arranged massacres and
burnings; massacres and burnings produced fierce re-
prisals. These caused the Penal Laws. The Penal Laws
lit the flames of insurrections. Insurrection led to war,
and to the absolute annexation of Ireland to England.”
This writer, who informs his Italian friends that the
Penal Laws were caused by Catholic Irish reprisals, and
other stuff of that sort, is sure to have some brilliant
impressions of us; and I mean to give my fellow Gaels
some specimens fortnightly, as he supplies them for the
consumption of his readers in Rome.

He tells us that as soon as he arrived in Dublin he
went with a friend named Patrick Flanagan, a leading
medical man, to see the Gaelic Leaguers assembled at
Kingsbridge to give Douglas Hyde a send off to
America. His friend Flanagan “ elbowed himself *’ and
this polite Italian through the crowd till they got near
Dr. Hyde as he addressed the throng—that is hard on a
leading Dublin physician.

will soon learn from this bit of dialogue which I
translate : —
